risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomisation was conducted using pre-generated random permuted blocks (block sizes of four and six). A data manager at the York Trials Unit, who was completely independent of the research team, created the randomisation programme, which was used to allocate participants to one of the two treatment arms" Comment: The paper explicitly states that allocation used pre-generated random permuted blocks, which is an acceptable random sequence generation method when the sequence is generated independently and programmatically. Although the exact algorithm is not named, the description is sufficient to judge the sequence as truly random.

Quote: "After completing the baseline assessment, the research nurse telephoned a secure and remote randomisation service, located at the York Trials Unit (University of York, UK)." Comment: This indicates central allocation via a secure remote randomisation service, which would prevent the enrolling nurse from foreseeing the next assignment before randomisation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Since assessment of wound healing is somewhat subjective and staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment, it would be important to conduct blinded outcome assessment in a full trial." Comment: The trial explicitly states that staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment allocation. Because the intervention was an obvious wound-care device versus standard care, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence care delivery, treatment changes, co-interventions, and participant-reported outcomes; therefore performance bias is high.

Quote: "A blinded outcome assessor (experienced Tissue Viability nurse not involved in the day-to-day conduct of the trial) then assessed photographs for (1) assessment of healing..." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly blinded and independent of trial conduct. The key healing outcome was assessed from digital photographs rather than direct bedside judgement, which reduces the chance that knowledge of allocation could influence measurement. Although participants and staff could not be blinded, the blinded photographic assessment makes detection bias for healing unlikely.

Quote: "There was no attrition recorded during this trial. From the 12 recruited participants, 5 ... exited the trial after completing the maximum 6-month follow-up. A further five participants ... completed less than the 6-month follow-up as they were recruited later into the study. Two participants died during the trial" Comment: Follow-up appears essentially complete for this pilot RCT, with no recorded loss to follow-up or withdrawal after randomisation. The shorter follow-up in some participants was due to later recruitment, not attrition, and the two deaths were reported as trial events rather than unexplained missing data. This makes incomplete outcome data unlikely to have biased the results.
